coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ning
Wang, Yiting
Guo, Tingting
Yang, Ting
Chen, Mingli
Wang, Jianhua
coredata.dc:description: A simple one-step hydrothermal green approach was reported for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) without any further decoration or modification with papaya powder as natural carbon source. In this economical and eco-friendly system, deionized water or 90% ethanol was used as solvent to produce water-soluble or ethanol-soluble CDs, respectively, termed as W-CDs and E-CDs. The quantum yield (QY) for W-CDs was 18.98%, while that for E-CDs was 18.39%. The potentials of the prepared carbon dots toward diverse applications were thoroughly investigated. W-CDs and E-CDs provide promising probes for fluorescence detection of Fe3+, offering limits of detection of 0.48μmolL−1 and 0.29μmolL−1, respectively. W-CDs was further demonstrated to be a promising probe for fluorescence sensing of Escherichia coli O157: H7, along with a limit of detection of 9.5×104 cfumL−1. Meanwhile, both W-CDs and E-CDs exhibit favorable biocompatibility, and demonstrated to be efficient for Hela cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green preparation
Carbon dots
Iron
E. coli sensor
Florescence
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of water and ethanol soluble carbon-dots
The fluorescence stability of W-CDs and E-CDs
Fluorescent sensing of Fe3+ and E.coli O157:H7
Characterization of the W-CDs and E-CDs
The stability of W-CDs and E-CDs
W-CDs and E-CDs as probes for Fe3+ sensing
E. coli sensing by use of W-CDs as probe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21375013, 21235001, 21475017 ), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N130105002, N140505003, N141008001 ) are highly appreciated. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XPS spectra of the water soluble CDs (W-CDs) (a) and the high resolution peaks for C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d). (B) XPS spectra of the ethanol soluble CDs (E-CDs) (a') and the high resolution peaks for C1s (b'), N1s (c') and O1s (d').
HRTEM images (a) and particle size distribution (b) of W-CDs, HRTEM images (c) and particle size distribution (d) of E-CDs.
The fluorescence spectra of W-CDs (0.94mgmL−1) and E-CDs (1.17mgmL−1) at different excitation wavelengths from 350 to 490nm.

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubating with 1mgmL−1 of W-CDs for 0h, 3h, 5h, 7h and 9h. 1st row: bright field images; 2nd row: the images with an excitation/emission wavelength at 340nm/420nm; 3rd row: the images with an excitation/emission wavelength at 495nm/520nm. The scale bar in all the images is 100µm.

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubating with 1mgmL−1 of E-CDs for 0h, 3h, 5h, 7h and 9h. 1st row: bright field images; 2nd row: the images with an excitation/emission wavelength at 340nm/420nm; 3rd row: the images with an excitation/emission wavelength at 495nm/520nm. The scale bar in all the images is 100µm.
Analytical results for iron content in Heme capsules (mg per capsule).

head.ce:title: Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple one-step hydrothermal green approach was reported for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) without any further decoration or modification with papaya powder as natural carbon source. In this economical and eco-friendly system, deionized water or 90% ethanol was used as solvent to produce water-soluble or ethanol-soluble CDs, respectively, termed as W-CDs and E-CDs. The quantum yield (QY) for W-CDs was 18.98%, while that for E-CDs was 18.39%. The potentials of the prepared carbon dots toward diverse applications were thoroughly investigated. W-CDs and E-CDs provide promising probes for fluorescence detection of Fe3+, offering limits of detection of 0.48μmolL−1 and 0.29μmolL−1, respectively. W-CDs was further demonstrated to be a promising probe for fluorescence sensing of Escherichia coli O157: H7, along with a limit of detection of 9.5×104 cfumL−1. Meanwhile, both W-CDs and E-CDs exhibit favorable biocompatibility, and demonstrated to be efficient for Hela cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots as small carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10nm (Xu et al., 2004) have emerged as important nanomaterials for the purpose of chemo- and bio-sensing (Shen and Xia, 2014; Xu et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2014), photocatalysis (Cao et al., 2011) and bioimaging (Mehta et al., 2014; Qian et al., 2015) due to their attractive fluorescence properties, including high quantum yield (QY), favorable photostability, high solubility in aqueous medium, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excitation-dependent emissions (Baker and Baker, 2010; Ding et al., 2014a; Jin et al., 2015). Carbon dots originated from various carbon sources (Li et al., 2013; Shen et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2013; Wang and Zhou, 2014) and surface functionalization attempts (Gao et al., 2013; Hou et al., 2015; Li et al., 2010; Maiti et al., 2013) by soft molecules have been demonstrated to be of great success for the purpose of bio-sensing and fluorescence imaging of biological cells, ranging from bacterial cells to animal cells (Lu et al., 2015; Meir et al., 2015; Wen et al., 2015).

Recently, green chemistry has become more and more attractive because of its clean and sustainable feature. Preparation of carbon dots using nontoxic solvents and economical green materials is the key issue that should be taken into consideration in a green synthetic strategy. It was reported that carica juice and pomelo peel serve as carbon precursors to prepare carbon dots with quantum yields of 7.0% and 6.9% (Kasibabu et al., 2015). Similarly, citrus limone juice in NaOH solution was used to produce CDs with a quantum yield of 12.1% (Oza et al., 2015). A nitrogen-doped carbon dots from silkworm chrysalis and the CDs were recently prepared and used for cell imaging (Feng et al., 2016). CDs have also been achieved by using pure honey as carbon source in the presence of H2O2 (Yang et al., 2014). In the preparations of CDs, the green materials were generally mixed with chemical reagents, e.g., acid, base or reductant (Mehta et al., 2014; Zhang and Chen, 2014). Wherein the selection of suitable carbon precursors is the key to improve or control the properties of carbon dots. A variety of reaction modes were used for carbon dots preparation, including laser ablation (Li et al., 2011), electrochemical (Deng et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2008), hydrothermal (Wu et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2012), solvothermal (Dong et al., 2013), microwave (Salinas-Castillo et al., 2013) and ultrasonic (Tang et al., 2012). Among them, hydrothermal reaction is promisingly attractive. It is safer to the environment and can generally lead to the functionalization of carbon dots surface by introducing carboxylic, amino and hydroxyl groups, and resulting in CDs with favorable stability and biocompatibility. This makes it easier for the conjugation of CDs with active species for specific biological applications (Jin et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013).

Ferric ion (Fe3+) is one of the most indispensable and abundant metal cations in natural environment, it plays crucial roles in biological systems (Lee et al., 2010). It may disturb the balance of cellular processes and the abnormal states may cause severe diseases like hemochromatosis, inflammation, heart failure, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ is of vital importance in biological applications. Due to the wide spread of multidrug-resistant E. coli and its serious threat to public health, the rapid and accurate detection of E. coli remains an important biological issue (Chen et al., 2015; Cheng et al., 2016).

In the present work, we demonstrate the use of a simple, economical and green approach for direct preparation of CDs with papaya as a natural carbon source without any further functionalization or modification of the CDs. The water soluble CDs (W-CDs) and ethanol soluble CDs (E-CDs) were obtained in deionized water and 90% ethanol respectively. E-CDs are more hydrophobic than W-CDs, and both W-CDs and E-CDs contain multiple functional groups, leading to their biocompatibility and extensive biological applications, as demonstrated by the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in Heme capsules with W-CDs and E-CDs as fluorescence probes. We have further demonstrated that the W-CDs provide a promising probe for the detection of E. coli O157:H7. In addition, both W-CDs and E-CDs are efficient probes for Hela cell imaging.

Papaya is obtained from Carrefour in Shenyang, China. The chemicals, NaHSO3, NaHSO4, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, KF, KIO3, sodium citrate, sodium acetate, acetic acid, sodium chloroacetate and phosphoric acid, were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The stocking solutions of a series of metal cations were prepared by using the corresponding metal chlorides or metal nitrates, e.g., HgCl2, AgNO3, CaCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, KCl, NaCl, MgCl2, CdCl2, BaCl2, NiCl2, Al(NO3)3 and Co(NO3)2. EDTA, NaHCO3, H3BO4, NaNO2 and NaNO3 were the products of Bodi Chemical Holiding Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). FeCl3, ethanol and quinine sulfate were received from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay kit was acquired from KeyGEN Biotech Co. (Nanjing, China). DMEM high glucose medium, fetal bovine serum, penicillin, streptomycin and 0.25% trypsin-EDTA were achieved from Hyclone (Thermo Scientific, USA). 18MΩcm−1 water was used throughout.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs were recorded by a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a 1.0-cm quartz cuvette at room temperature. FT-IR spectra were obtained on a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (ThermoElectron, USA) using a KBr disk from 500 to 4000cm−1. XPS analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo Electron, England). Surface charge property and size distribution of CDs were investigated with a Zetasizer Nano ZS/ZEN3690 (Malvern, England). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100 (HR) transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells were taken on an inverted fluorescent microscope (Nikon, Japan). MTT assay was measured using a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader at 490nm (BioTek, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of water and ethanol soluble carbon-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Papaya peel was removed, and the flesh was cut into small pieces, i.e., 3mm×3mm×3mm. The papaya flesh was dried at −54°C in vacuum for 24h. The mixture of papaya pieces (0.4g) and deionized water (10mL) was set into a Teflon-lined pressure vessel of 25-mL capacity and heated at 200°C for 5h, until a uniform brown liquid product was obtained, which indicates the formation of water soluble carbon dots (W-CDs). The ethanol soluble CDs (E-CDs) were prepared with a same procedure by replacing the 10mL deionized water with a same volume of ethanol (90%, v/v). The brown products were taken out and allowed to cool to ambient temperature. They are then filtered and collected by further purification with dialysis against cellulose ester membrane (MWCO: 500–1000) for 72h.

The quantum yields (QYs) of CDs were determined by a relative method (Mehta et al., 2014). The aqueous solution of quinine sulfate was diluted with 0.1molL−1 H2SO4 as the reference solution, while those of W-CDs and E-CDs were diluted with deionized water, to keep their absorption at a minimum, i.e., <0.05, at the excitation wavelength of 370nm in order to minimize their re-absorption. QY was derived based on the following equation, with φ
X and φ
S (0.54) as QY of CDs and quinine sulfate, I
X and I
S as their emission area, η
X (1.33) and η
S (1.33) as their refractive index, A
X and A
S as their optical densities.
φ
X
=
φ
S
A
S
A
X
I
X
I
S
[
η
X
η
S
]
2

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence stability of W-CDs and E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH dependence on fluorescence behavior of the obtained CDs was investigated in BR buffer solutions (0.04molL−1 boric acid, 0.04molL−1 acetic acid, 0.04molL−1 phosphoric acid and sodium hydroxide), while the influence of ionic strength was evaluated in the presence of various concentrations of NaCl (0–1.0molL−1). In addition, for the purpose of evaluating the influence of different cationic and anionic species on the fluorescence of the W-CDs and E-CDs, their fluorescence stability was studied in the presence of various salts at same concentration level of 10mmolL−1. In all the cases, the CDs are excited at 370nm and their fluorescence emission at 450nm with the slit of 5nm were recorded for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Fe3+ and E.coli O157:H7
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, a procedure for Fe3+ detection was developed as shortly described herein. The suspension of W-CDs (0.94mgmL−1) and E-CDs (1.17mgmL−1) are prepared at room temperature. At pH 3, 175μL of CDs solution and a same volume of Fe3+ solution with different concentrations (25–700μmolL−1 for W-CDs and 10–800μmolL−1 for E-CDs) are added into a 2-mL centrifuge tube for facilitating their reaction. A blank control is prepared at same conditions in the absence of Fe3+. For the assay of low concentration of Fe3+, 100μL of CDs solution and 300μL of Fe3+ solution (1–10μmolL−1 for W-CDs and 1–8μmolL−1 for E-CDs, respectively) are added into a 2-mL centrifuge tube. The reaction systems were excited at 370nm and the corresponding fluorescence emissions at 450nm were recorded.

For fluorescence sensing of E. coli., the original E. coli O157:H7 (at a level of 1×108 cfumL−1) is washed with PBS buffer (0.2molL−1, pH 7.0) for twice to remove the broth and it was then diluted to 105–108 cfumL−1. 1000μL of this solution and 500μL of CDs solution are added into a 5-mL centrifuge tube, which is then incubated at 37°C for 1h. Thereafter, the bacteria suspension is centrifuged at 8000rpm for 5min followed by washing for twice with PBS buffer (0.2molL−1, pH 7). The bacteria were then re-suspended in PBS buffer (0.2molL−1, pH 7) of 400μL. In all the cases, CDs are excited at 370nm and their fluorescence emission at 425nm with a slit of 10nm was recorded for the purpose of quantification.

HeLa cells were used as model for fluorescence imaging. The cells were cultured in DMEM medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100unitsmL−1 penicillin and 100mgmL−1 streptomycin at 37°C in a 5% CO2 humidified incubator for 24h. For in vitro imaging, DMEM medium was removed and the cells were washed with PBS buffer (10mmolL−1, pH 7.4) for three times. Subsequently, the cells were incubated in a medium containing 1mgmL−1 CDs filtered through a 0.2µm sterilized filter membrane. After further incubating with CDs for 3h, 5h, 7h and 9h, the cells were washed with PBS buffer (10mmolL−1, pH 7.4) for three times to remove free CDs from the culture medium. The fluorescence images were taken on an inverted fluorescent microscope with laser excitation at 340nm and 495nm.

Methylthiazolydiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of CDs. At first, HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well culture plate in DMEM medium. After incubation for 12h, CDs with various concentrations (0, 1.0, 10, 50, 100, 500 and 700mgL−1, in DMEM medium, at pH 7.4) were added into the cells and incubated for 20h at 37°C with 5% CO2 and 100% humidity. 20μL of MTT was then added into each well and the plate was further incubated for additional 4h. Afterwards the medium was removed and 100μL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve formazan. Control experiments were measured under same incubation conditions in the absence of CDs. The absorbance of each well was measured at 490nm with a ELISA plate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the W-CDs and E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectra of the native papaya powder, the water and ethanol soluble carbon dots, W-CDs and E-CDs, as illustrated in Fig. S1 revealed that there exist N-H, -COO, C-O and C-H functional groups in the surface of both the papaya and the CDs. The results further indicated that both W-CDs and E-CDs remain the functional groups as identified in the native papaya, i.e., the absorption bands of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations appeared within 3130–3540cm−1, meanwhile C-H stretching vibration was identified from 2870 to 2940cm−1. C=O and C=C stretching vibrations are found at 1720 and 1630cm−1 (Yang et al., 2014), while that of C-N stretching was recorded at 1400cm−1 (Jiang et al., 2015). The absorptions within 1050–1100cm−1 were associated with C-O bending vibrations (Kumar et al., 2015).

XPS spectra were recorded to investigate surface state of W-CDs and E-CDs ( Fig. 1). The spectrum of W-CDs shows three typical peaks of C1s (284.6eV), N1s (399.7eV) and O1s (531.9eV). Elemental analysis revealed the composition of W-CDs to be C (74.1wt%), N (1.7wt%) and O (23.5wt%). High resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1(b)) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.5eV, 285.2eV, 286.3eV and 288.2eV, corresponding to C1s states in C–C/C=C, C–N, C–O and O–C=O bonds. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1(c)) displays two peaks at 399.7eV and 400.7eV, which can be attributed to C-N-C and N-H. O1s (Fig. 1(d)) exhibits two main bands at 531.9eV and 532.9eV, confirming C-OH/C-O-C and C=O, respectively (Ding et al., 2014b; Jiang et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2015).

Similarly, the spectrum of E-CDs shows three peaks of C1s (284.6eV), N1s (399.3eV) and O1s (531.1eV), with contents of C (71.8wt%), N (2.0wt%) and O (26.1wt%). C1s spectrum (Fig. 1(b)') shows four peaks at 284.6eV, 285.6eV, 286.3eV and 288.1eV, associating with C–C/C=C, C–N, C–O and O–C=O bonds. N1s spectrum (Fig. 1(c)') displays two peaks at 399.3eV and 400.5eV, corresponding to C-N-C and N-H. O1s (Fig. 1(d)') exhibits two main bands at 531.4eV and 532.7eV, attributes to C-OH/C-O-C and C=O.

HRTEM in Fig. 2 illustrates that both W-CDs and E-CDs are well dispersed in aqueous medium with diameter distribution within 2–6nm and 8–18nm, resulting in average particle sizes of ca. 3.4nm and 10.8nm. W-CDs are significantly smaller than E-CDs due to solvent effect during the preparation process. In aqueous medium, there exist more saccharides and some water soluble micromolecule, it facilitates the production of compact CDs. While in 90% ethanol, the presence of more organic macromolecules templates a larger size for the E-CDs.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of W-CDs and E-CDs were illustrated in Fig. S2. Due to electron transfer of the nanocarbon structure, UV–vis absorption spectrum shows a peak at around 250–290nm, ascribing to the presence of aromatic π orbitals or n-p* transition of carbon dots (Yang et al., 2014). Meanwhile, both W-CDs and E-CDs give rise to high photoluminescence with a maximum at 450nm by excitation at 370nm. Upon irradiation at 365nm, the carbon dots solution can immediately generate a blue color (Fig. S2 inset).

The fluorescence spectra display the characteristic feature of CDs that the emission peak shifts from 445nm to 530nm by varying the excitation wavelength within 350–490nm ( Fig. 3). That is to say the CDs obtained exhibit different emission colors, e.g., blue, green and red, by manipulating the excitation wavelength. This phenomenon is widely observed in luminescent carbon nanomaterials, caused by the optical selection of different surface defect states near the Fermi level of CDs (Cao et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of W-CDs and E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S3 shows that both W-CDs and E-CDs exhibit stable photoluminescence within pH 3–10. However, in a strong acidic or basic medium, pH<2 and pH>10, a sharp decrease of fluorescence intensity is encountered. Meanwhile, zeta potentials of W-CDs and E-CDs were measured to be −10.37±0.12mV at pH 5 and −5.60±0.13mV at pH 5.6, respectively. This pH-dependent photoluminescent behavior is due to the fact that CDs serve as electron donors and electron acceptors. However, H+ or OH− may interrupt or inhibit the electron transfer process (Liang et al., 2013).

The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of W-CDs and E-CDs was investigated by incorporating 0–2.0molL−1 NaCl in the solution of carbon dots (Fig. S4). Ionic strength at a certain level poses virtually no effect on the photoluminescence of W-CDs and E-CDs. Moreover, it is obvious that E-CDs solution is more stable, in which no obvious change of fluorescent intensity can be observed after illumination for 2h (Fig. S5).

The experiments further indicated that the presence of various anionic species at a certain concentration, e.g., 10mmolL−1 of HSO3 −, HSO4 −, HCO3 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, ClCH2COO−, CH3COO−, IO3 −, F−, Cl−, BF4 −, NO2 − and NO3 −, cause no obvious change on the fluorescence intensity of W-CDs and E-CDs (Fig. S6). The above observations are favorable for the treatment and sensing in biological systems with W-CDs and E-CDs, where various anionic and cationic species are frequently encountered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: W-CDs and E-CDs as probes for Fe3+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing of Fe3+ with W-CDs and E-CDs as probes were evaluated in an aqueous medium at pH 3 as adjusted by use of dilute hydrochloric acid to avoid hydrolysis of metal cations. With the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+, a decline on the fluorescence emission at 450nm is observed and a linear calibration was recorded within a certain range by plotting Fe3+ concentration versus net difference of fluorescence, i.e., F 0 −F, where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. It is further demonstrated that the linear ranges for Fe3+ sensing could be regulated by changing on the amount of W-CDs and E-CDs used in the sensing system. Fig. S7 indicated that the use of 100μL and 175μL of W-CDs at 0.94mgmL−1 results in Fe3+ linear calibrations within two concentration ranges, i.e., 1–10μmolL−1 and 25–700μmolL−1, respectively, giving rise to a limit of detection of 0.48μmolL−1. Similarly, the use of 100μL and 175μL of E-CDs at 1.17mgmL−1 provides two linear ranges for Fe3+, that is 1–8μmolL−1 and 10–800μmolL−1, along with a limit of detection of 0.29μmolL−1 (Fig. S8).

It has been reported that carbon dots are able to act as a probe for the sensing of transition metal species due to the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs. In the present case, both W-CDs and E-CDs contain ample oxygen containing functional groups, e.g., -OH and -COOH, which facilitates the formation of complexes with Fe3+, and thus makes Fe3+ sensing feasible (Huang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2014; Qu et al., 2013). On the other hand, the content of oxygen containing functional groups in E-CDs is higher than that in W-CDs, and thus a little more sensitive detection of Fe3+ by E-CDs is observed.

For evaluating the practical usefulness of the present Fe3+ sensing approach, it has been applied for the determination of iron in a real sample matrix, i.e., Heme capsules (Guoguang Pharmaceutical Co. Jiangxi, China). The sample pretreatment is performed as in the following: 0.45g of sample powder was dissolved in 2mL of diluted HCl and it was stirred for 10min Afterwards, the mixture was filtered through a 0.2µm membrane. The sample solution was then diluted to 400mL and the acidity was controlled at pH 3. The results were shown in Table 1. It can be seen that the results achieved by both W-CDs and E-CDs as the probe are comparable with that obtained by Phenanthroline spectrophotometry (Malik and Rao, 1997).

In the detection of iron with W-CDs and E-CDs as probes, the selectivity against various coexisting metal cations is among the most important issues for applying to real world samples, especially biological samples. For this purpose, the following metal cations, Co2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+ and Ca2+, were introduced into the sensing system to evaluate their potential interfering effects on iron sensing. The results in Fig. S9 shown that both sensing systems with W-CDs and E-CDs as probes exhibit favorable selectivity to Fe3+ against most of the coexisting metal cations at a relatively higher concentration level of 10mmolL−1. The results indicated that although Co2+, Hg2+ and Ni2+ exhibit a small extent of interference, their concentrations in real samples or sample digests are generally significantly lower than 10mmolL−1, and thus in practical applications their interfering effects are well below the control limit and thus are safely negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: E. coli sensing by use of W-CDs as probe
ce:section.ce:para: For biological analytical applications, it is highly desired to provide a simple approach for rapid and accurate evaluation of the contamination by bacteria. In this respect, both the water and ethanol soluble carbon dots have been examined for their potential as probes in the assay or sensing of E. coli O157:H7. The experiments demonstrated that E. coli O157:H7 caused a significant enhancement effect on the fluorescence emission of W-CDs and a linear calibration was achieved within a range of 105–108 cfumL−1 (Fig. S10), giving rise to a limit of detection of 9.5×104 cfumL−1. This provides a novel approach for the sensing of E. coli O157:H7 with W-CDs as a fluorescence probe. As a contrary, however, the presence of E. coli O157:H7 within the same concentration range caused no obvious response on the fluorescence of E-CDs. This indicated that E-CDs are unable to serve as a probe for the sensing of E. coli O157:H7.

The above observations are attributed to the fact that carbon dots achieved in aqueous medium contain saccharides, i.e., mannose, which tend to exhibit strong interaction with FimH (Huang et al., 2009; Weng et al., 2015). That is to say the FimH proteins on the tip of the fimbriae of E. coli O157:H7 interact with mannose in the water soluble carbon dots (W-CDs) to produce fluorescence change of W-CDs, providing the basis for quantitative sensing of E.coli O157:H7. On the other hand, however, the carbon dots obtained in ethanol (E-CDs) contain organic macromolecules, which is consistent with the observation of a larger size for E-CDs as described in the previous section. E-CDs offer no obvious interaction with E. coli O157:H7, and thus makes them unable to probe E. coli O157:H7.

Cellular cytotoxicity of the water and ethanol soluble carbon dots is evaluated by using human HeLa cells in a MTT assay and the results were given in Fig. S11. It is seen that both W-CDs and E-CDs are of low toxicity to HeLa cells, giving rise to a cell viability of >95% at a concentration of 50mgL−1. Moreover, a favorable cell viability of >90% is still maintained at a high concentration of CDs up to 500mgL−1.

The capability for cell imaging by W-CDs and E-CDs is investigated by using HeLa cells as a model. Fig. 4 illustrated the cell imaging results after incubating HeLa cells with 1mgmL−1 of W-CDs for 0h, 3h, 5h, 7h and 9h. It is obviously seen that with laser excitation/emission wavelength at 340nm/420nm and 495nm/520nm respectively, bright blue (2nd row) and green (3rd row) fluorescent emissions were clearly recorded on the fluorescent microscope. Fig. 5 demonstrated the HeLa cell imaging results after incubating them with 1mgmL−1 E-CDs for 0h, 3h, 5h, 7h and 9h. Similar results were observed as those for the W-CDs by excitation/emission wavelength at 340nm/420nm and 495nm/520nm. However, it is clearly seen that water soluble CDs give rise to better imaging results as compared to E-CDs probably due to the fact that W-CDs enter the cells more quickly with respect to E-CDs, because of the smaller size of W-CDs. The observations described herein well demonstrate that W-CDs and E-CDs with low cytotoxicity are promising for the applications in bio-imaging.

A green carbon source, papaya powder, is demonstrated to be efficient for the preparation of water and ethanol soluble carbon dots, which give rise to favorable fluorescence quantum yields and provide promising probes for direct sensing of iron(III) without any further chemical modification. The water soluble CDs also provide a novel approach for fluorescence sensing of E. coli O157:H7. In addition, both water and ethanol soluble carbon dots are biocompatible and demonstrated to be efficient for HeLa cell imaging. In general, the present work provides a new and green avenue for the production of fluorescent carbon dots for various biological applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21375013, 21235001, 21475017), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N130105002, N140505003, N141008001) are highly appreciated.
